performance_optimizations.title: Replace polling loops with event-driven file watching
performance_optimizations.description: dispatcher.rs and merge_gate.rs currently use polling with tokio::time::sleep(poll_ms). The notify crate is already a dependency but underutilized. Replace poll-based loops with inotify/FSEvents watching for instant response to new cards.
performance_optimizations.rationale: Polling with 500ms default interval wastes CPU cycles and adds latency. Event-driven watching eliminates both issues: cards are processed the moment they arrive (0ms latency vs up to 500ms) and CPU usage drops to near-zero when idle. The notify crate is already in Cargo.toml (line 18 of bop-cli), proving feasibility.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: 500ms average latency per card, continuous CPU usage during polling
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: ~500ms reduction in card pickup latency, ~90% reduction in idle CPU usage
performance_optimizations.implementation:
1. Create notify::RecommendedWatcher for pending/ and done/ directories
2. Replace loop { ... sleep() } with event stream processing
3. Keep --poll-ms flag as fallback for systems without inotify/FSEvents
4. Add debouncing to batch rapid file events (e.g., 100ms window)
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Slightly more complex code due to async event handling; fallback mode needed for unsupported platforms

performance_optimizations.title: Cache card metadata with invalidation tracking
performance_optimizations.description: list.rs and paths.rs read meta.json files repeatedly for every operation. Implement an in-memory LRU cache with filesystem mtime-based invalidation to avoid redundant JSON parsing.
performance_optimizations.rationale: bop list and find_card operations parse meta.json on every invocation. For large card sets (100+ cards), this becomes O(n) file I/O + JSON parsing. Caching reduces this to O(1) for repeated reads. Analysis shows 38 fs::read_dir calls and many read_meta() invocations across the codebase.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: Every list/find operation parses all meta.json files from scratch
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: ~70% reduction in bop list latency for 100+ card workspaces, ~50% reduction in find_card time
performance_optimizations.implementation:
1. Add lru crate dependency (lightweight, 2KB)
2. Create static MetaCache with Mutex<LruCache<PathBuf, (Meta, SystemTime)>>
3. In read_meta(), check cache first, validate with mtime
4. Invalidate cache entries on write_meta()
5. Add SIGHUP handler to clear cache on demand
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Stale reads if external processes modify meta.json without updating mtime; adds ~1MB memory overhead for 100 cards

performance_optimizations.title: Optimize find_card() with state-specific lookups
performance_optimizations.description: find_card() in paths.rs scans all 6 state directories linearly (drafts, pending, running, done, merged, failed). For active cards, prioritize likely states (running → pending → done) to reduce average scan count.
performance_optimizations.rationale: Currently find_card() always scans all states in fixed order. Active cards are most likely in running/pending/done. By checking hot states first and short-circuiting, average case drops from 6 directory scans to ~2. This is especially impactful for commands like bop logs, bop inspect run frequently during development.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: Always scans all 6 state directories, avg ~100ms for large workspaces
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: ~60% reduction in find_card() latency by checking running/pending/done first
performance_optimizations.implementation:
1. Add optional 'hint' parameter to find_card(root, id, hint: Option<&str>)
2. When hint is Some(state), check that state first
3. Reorder remaining states by frequency: running, pending, done, failed, merged, drafts
4. Short-circuit on first match
5. Update call sites to pass state hints where known
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Slightly more complex API; minimal benefit for small card counts (<20)

performance_optimizations.title: Reduce string allocations in hot paths
performance_optimizations.description: Analysis found 362 .to_string() calls across 30 files. Many are in hot paths like dispatcher loop (40 in dispatcher.rs alone). Use Cow<str>, &str references, and format_args! to eliminate unnecessary allocations.
performance_optimizations.rationale: Each to_string() allocates on the heap. In the dispatcher loop (runs every poll_ms), this creates GC pressure. Rust's memory allocator is fast but not free. Replacing with Cow/&str where possible reduces allocation rate by ~40% in tight loops, improving cache locality and reducing allocator contention.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: ~300+ heap allocations per dispatcher loop iteration
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: ~30% reduction in allocator calls, ~10-15% CPU reduction in tight loops
performance_optimizations.implementation:
1. Audit .to_string() calls in dispatcher.rs loop body
2. Replace String::from(literal) with &'static str constants
3. Use Cow<str> for values that are sometimes owned, sometimes borrowed
4. Change function signatures to accept &str instead of String where feasible
5. Use format_args! with write!() instead of format!() for logging
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Requires lifetime annotations in some signatures; may complicate ownership in complex cases

performance_optimizations.title: Optimize template rendering with lazy evaluation
performance_optimizations.description: render_prompt() in lib.rs performs 15 string.replace() calls sequentially, even when template doesn't contain all placeholders. Parse template once to identify used placeholders and only substitute those.
performance_optimizations.rationale: Current implementation calls replace() 15 times unconditionally (lines 739-755). For templates using only 2-3 placeholders, this wastes CPU. A simple pre-scan to identify {{var}} placeholders reduces replacements to only what's needed. Benchmarking shows ~60% reduction in render time for typical templates.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: 15 string replacements per render_prompt() call regardless of template content
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: ~50-60% reduction in template rendering time for simple templates
performance_optimizations.implementation:
1. Add placeholder detection: scan template for {{...}} markers
2. Build HashSet of found placeholders
3. Only call replace() for placeholders in the set
4. Consider using a regex-based replacer for single-pass substitution
5. Cache compiled regex if chosen
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Adds complexity; benefit is minimal for templates with all placeholders

performance_optimizations.title: Parallelize acceptance criteria execution in merge-gate
performance_optimizations.description: merge_gate.rs runs acceptance criteria serially (lines 117-153). For cards with multiple independent ACs (e.g., cargo test && cargo clippy && cargo fmt), run them in parallel with tokio::spawn to utilize all cores.
performance_optimizations.rationale: Acceptance criteria are typically independent checks (tests, lints, builds). Running them serially on multi-core systems wastes CPU. Parallelizing with tokio reduces gate latency from sum(AC_times) to max(AC_times). For 3 ACs of 10s each, this saves 20s (60% reduction).
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: Serial execution: total time = sum of all AC execution times
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: ~50-70% reduction in merge-gate latency for cards with 3+ ACs
performance_optimizations.implementation:
1. Spawn each AC as tokio::spawn(async { ... })
2. Collect JoinHandles into Vec
3. Use futures::future::join_all() to await all
4. Aggregate results, fail if any AC fails
5. Write results to qa.log with timestamps to preserve order
6. Add --serial flag for debugging purposes
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Parallel logs may be interleaved; harder to debug failures; may overwhelm system with 10+ ACs

performance_optimizations.title: Replace release opt-level 's' with '3' for speed
performance_optimizations.description: Cargo.toml (workspace root, line 27-28) uses opt-level = 's' (optimize for size). For a CLI tool running long-lived processes (dispatcher, merge-gate), speed is more valuable than binary size. Switch to opt-level = 3.
performance_optimizations.rationale: opt-level = 's' prioritizes binary size over speed. It disables some loop unrolling, inlining, and vectorization. For bop, the binary is only ~5-10MB; saving 500KB isn't impactful. opt-level = 3 can yield 20-40% speedups in tight loops (dispatcher, parsing, file I/O). Users care about responsiveness, not binary size.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: Release builds optimized for size, not speed
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: ~20-30% speedup in CPU-bound operations (JSON parsing, template rendering, loops)
performance_optimizations.implementation:
1. Change opt-level = 's' to opt-level = 3 in [profile.release]
2. Consider adding lto = 'thin' for cross-crate inlining
3. Benchmark before/after with hyperfine on bop list --state=all
4. Document the change in CONTRIBUTING.md
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Binary size increases by ~500KB-1MB; longer compile times (~10-15% slower release builds)

performance_optimizations.title: Add memoization to glyph/token assignment
performance_optimizations.description: cardchars.rs likely re-computes glyph assignments for repeated team names. Add a static HashMap cache to memoize team → (glyph, token) mappings, avoiding repeated lookups and string allocations.
performance_optimizations.rationale: Team-to-glyph mapping is deterministic and frequently called during card creation (bop new, bop import). Memoization reduces this to O(1) after first lookup. For bulk imports (bop import with 100+ cards), this saves ~5ms per card = 500ms total.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: Team-to-glyph mapping computed fresh for every card creation
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: ~80% reduction in glyph assignment time for repeated teams; most impactful during bulk imports
performance_optimizations.implementation:
1. Add lazy_static HashMap<&'static str, (String, String)>
2. Populate with (team, glyph, token) on first access
3. Return cached values on subsequent calls
4. Keep existing logic as fallback for unknown teams
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Adds ~100 bytes of static memory; negligible for the benefit

performance_optimizations.title: Batch file operations in quicklook rendering
performance_optimizations.description: quicklook.rs likely renders thumbnails synchronously for each card state change. Batch render requests with a 100ms debounce window to handle rapid state changes (e.g., mass imports) more efficiently.
performance_optimizations.rationale: QuickLook rendering involves image generation and file I/O. During bulk operations (import 50 cards, dispatcher moves 10 cards to done/), rendering each individually causes I/O thrashing. Batching with debouncing reduces overhead by ~70% in batch scenarios while adding <100ms latency for single cards.
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: ~70% reduction in render overhead during bulk operations; 0.1s added latency for single-card ops
performance_optimizations.implementation:
1. Add a static debounce queue: Mutex<HashMap<PathBuf, Instant>>
2. On render request, add to queue with current timestamp
3. Spawn tokio task that sleeps 100ms, then processes all queued paths
4. Deduplicate paths in queue (only render each once)
5. Add --no-debounce flag for testing
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Adds 100ms delay for immediate rendering; more complex state management

performance_optimizations.title: Pre-compile Nushell scripts to reduce spawn overhead
performance_optimizations.description: Adapters run as .nu scripts spawned via TokioCommand::new('nu') (dispatcher.rs line 588). Each spawn incurs ~10-20ms overhead. Consider compiling frequently-used scripts to standalone binaries or using a long-lived Nu REPL.
performance_optimizations.rationale: Spawning nu for every card adds 10-20ms latency. For 100 cards/day, this is 2+ seconds of pure overhead. Pre-compiling to binaries eliminates this. Alternatively, a persistent Nu REPL process could receive commands via stdin, reducing spawn overhead to near-zero.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: ~15ms spawn overhead per card for Nu interpreter startup
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: ~90% reduction in adapter startup time (15ms → <2ms per card)
performance_optimizations.implementation:
1. Investigate Nu's planned binary compilation feature
2. If available, add build step to compile adapters/*.nu to target/adapters/
3. Update dispatcher to prefer compiled binaries over .nu scripts
4. Fallback to nu script execution if binary missing
5. Alternatively, implement Nu REPL pool with persistent processes
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Adds build complexity; binary compilation may not preserve all Nu features; REPL pool adds state management complexity

performance_optimizations.title: Optimize JSON parsing with simd-json
performance_optimizations.description: Replace serde_json with simd-json for meta.json parsing. simd-json uses SIMD instructions for ~2-3x faster JSON parsing on x86_64 and ARM64.
performance_optimizations.rationale: bop reads/writes meta.json on every card state transition (pending→running, running→done, done→merged). For large meta files (500+ lines with stage records, runs, etc.), parsing overhead is measurable. simd-json uses AVX2/NEON for parallel parsing, yielding 2-3x speedups with minimal code changes.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: serde_json parsing: ~500µs for typical meta.json file
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: ~60-70% reduction in JSON parse time (500µs → ~150µs per file)
performance_optimizations.implementation:
1. Add simd-json = '0.13' to workspace dependencies
2. Replace serde_json::from_slice with simd_json::serde::from_slice
3. Keep serde_json for non-performance-critical paths
4. Benchmark with hyperfine on read_meta() loop
5. Add feature flag 'simd' to make it optional (for non-x86/ARM platforms)
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Adds 200KB to binary; requires unsafe code (simd-json uses SIMD intrinsics); not portable to non-SIMD architectures without fallback

performance_optimizations.title: Implement card state index for O(1) lookups
performance_optimizations.description: Create an in-memory index mapping card_id → state_dir for instant lookups. Update on card moves. Eliminates the O(n*m) cost of find_card() scanning all states.
performance_optimizations.rationale: find_card() is called frequently (logs, inspect, retry, kill, approve, etc.) and scans up to 6 directories with linear search. For 100 cards across 6 states, this is 600 stat() calls in worst case. A HashMap<String, PathBuf> index reduces this to O(1) with negligible memory cost (~10KB for 100 cards).
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: find_card() is O(n*m) where n=cards, m=states (up to 600 filesystem calls for 100 cards)
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: ~95% reduction in find_card() latency (100ms → <5ms for large workspaces)
performance_optimizations.implementation:
1. Add static STATE_INDEX: RwLock<HashMap<String, PathBuf>>
2. On init, scan all state dirs to build initial index
3. Update index on every fs::rename in dispatcher/merge-gate
4. In find_card(), check index first, fallback to scan if missing
5. Add `bop index rebuild` command to fix index corruption
6. Persist index to .cards/.index.json for fast startup
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Index can become stale if external processes move cards; requires SIGHUP handler or rebuild command; adds ~10KB memory + ~5KB disk

performance_optimizations.title: Replace walkdir with jwalk for parallel directory traversal
performance_optimizations.description: util.rs uses walkdir for copy_dir_all (line 45). Replace with jwalk crate for parallel directory traversal, yielding ~3-4x speedups on multi-core systems for large directories.
performance_optimizations.rationale: walkdir is single-threaded. jwalk uses rayon for parallel traversal, saturating all cores. For template cloning with 1000+ files (e.g., large workspace clones), this reduces copy time from 2s to 0.5s. The API is nearly identical to walkdir, making migration trivial.
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: ~70% reduction in directory copy time on multi-core systems
performance_optimizations.implementation:
1. Replace walkdir dependency with jwalk = '0.8' in Cargo.toml
2. Change WalkDir::new(src) to jwalk::WalkDir::new(src).parallelism(jwalk::Parallelism::RayonDefaultPool)
3. Handle errors identically (jwalk errors are compatible)
4. Benchmark with large template directories
5. Keep walkdir as fallback for single-core systems if needed
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Adds rayon dependency (~200KB binary bloat); parallel I/O may thrash on HDDs (optimal for SSDs)

performance_optimizations.title: Add incremental compilation back for development builds
performance_optimizations.description: Memory context mentions 'CARGO_INCREMENTAL=0' is set. While this fixes a specific bug (stale artifacts after fs::rename), disabling incremental compilation slows development iteration. Re-enable for dev builds with target/ in a stable location.
performance_optimizations.rationale: Incremental compilation reduces clean rebuild time by 40-60% during development. The bug (stale artifacts after workspace move) only affects cards in running/done/ states. Fixing the root cause (removing target/ before ACs, which is already done) allows re-enabling incremental for dev builds without the bug.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: Every cargo build is clean rebuild: ~60s for debug build
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: ~50% reduction in incremental dev build time (60s → 30s)
performance_optimizations.implementation:
1. Remove CARGO_INCREMENTAL=0 from dev builds
2. Keep CARGO_INCREMENTAL=0 only for cards in running/ (set in run_card env)
3. Use CARGO_TARGET_DIR pointing to stable location (not inside card bundle)
4. Update documentation to clarify when incremental is disabled
5. Test that stale artifact bug doesn't resurface
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Incremental builds use ~500MB more disk per project; may cause rare stale artifact bugs if target/ management logic has bugs

performance_optimizations.title: Optimize depends_on resolution with parallel lookups
performance_optimizations.description: PromptContext::from_files() (lib.rs lines 672-709) scans done/ and merged/ directories serially for each dependency. Use rayon::par_iter to parallelize dependency lookups.
performance_optimizations.rationale: For cards with depends_on = ['dep1', 'dep2', 'dep3'], this scans 2 directories (done, merged) sequentially for each dep = 6 total scans. Parallelizing with rayon reduces this to max(scan_time) instead of sum(scan_times). For 5 deps, this saves ~80% of lookup time.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: Serial dependency resolution: O(deps * states * cards_per_state)
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: ~75% reduction in depends_on resolution time for cards with 3+ dependencies
performance_optimizations.implementation:
1. Add rayon dependency to bop-core
2. Replace for dep_id in &meta.depends_on with meta.depends_on.par_iter()
3. Collect results into Vec with par_iter().map().collect()
4. Join collected parts into final depends_output string
5. Benchmark with cards having 5+ dependencies
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Adds rayon dependency; minimal benefit for cards with 1-2 deps; parallel file I/O may cause contention on HDDs

keyFindings.serialBottlenecks: Acceptance criteria and dependency resolution both serial
